reason: The item cleanly targets boundary-aware engineering judgment: separating onset sensitivity from post-onset severity and from pre-runaway diagnosability, with correct options staying within those stated observations. Decoys are plausible but lean heavily on the same overreliance-on-a-single-metric failure pattern, slightly reducing discriminative sharpness versus experts who will quickly reject them.

The item cleanly tests evidence-bounded safety reasoning across aging, SOC severity, soot hazard, and confined-space gas accumulation, with correct options closely anchored to the stated observations. Decoys are plausible and primarily fail by overclaiming (elimination of hazard; non-hazardous soot), creating meaningful separation for experts who respect boundary conditions. Minor risk remains from the multi-select, multi-factor structure and slight wording/absolutism cues that can make some distractors easier to eliminate than intended.

The prompt tightly constrains the intended inference: capacity-dependent (non-portable) normalized gas release and the need to control a mechanical preload condition to make gas-generation characterization representative for vent sizing. It has good diagnostic value because it penalizes two common engineering mistakes—porting small-cell L/Ah values to larger formats and ignoring/underspecifying module compression effects—while still requiring boundary care (not transferring the 2 kN threshold numerically). The main quality limitation is structural: as a short-answer item without decoy options, it provides less controlled discrimination and more grading latitude than a well-constructed multi-choice variant.
